Lab notebook · Anya · May 2026
n=247 configurations · 15 seeds × 16 probes · IQ4_XS · plus a 30-layer MoE sibling
A surgical record

Gemma 4,
opened up at every layer.

A 42-layer language model — Gemma 4 E4B — is duplicated, pruned, swapped and recombined one residual block at a time. Most of these surgeries make it worse. A few make it better, and the pattern of which-is-which turns out to be almost entirely explained by where the model stashes its keys and values. Then the same scalpel meets a larger, stranger patient — the 26B-A4B mixture-of-experts — which refuses to respond the way the dense sibling does. Why it refuses is the most useful thing the bigger specimen has to teach.

42Transformer layers
[5 sliding + 1 global] × 7
247Configurations
swept end-to-end
2Anchor layers
L22 sliding · L23 global
18Shared-KV consumers
L24 – L41
3.5×Best lift over baseline
strict-correct rate
Provenance

On April 11, 2026, a discussion thread opened on the Gemma 4 31B model card: could someone with the hardware run David Ng's RYS layer-duplication technique on it? Ng — who created RYS and rode it to the top of the open LLM leaderboard — estimated about 40 H100-hours and said he'd get to it when compute allowed. The thread went quiet; the 31B never got its surgery. (Ng has since returned to RYS on the current model generation — see his 2026 retrospective, linked below.)

Meanwhile — across April and May — this experiment ran on a Mac Mini M4 with 16 GB of unified memory, sweeping 247 surgical configurations on the smaller E4B sibling. It is the first known working RYS sweep on the Gemma 4 family — unblocked by reverse-engineering three undocumented per-layer metadata arrays that the upstream tooling silently leaves stale after a duplication. The original technique was for somebody with H100s. This one was for somebody with a kitchen.

Retail

§1The patient

Gemma 4 E4B is a small open-weights model published by Google, with one architectural feature that turns out to dominate everything else for this work: it does not actually carry its own keys and values in the back half of the network. Layers 0–22 are writers that compute their own Q/K/V projections. Layers 24–41 are consumers: at inference time the engine routes them to read from a two-slot dictionary written by exactly two layers — L22 (the sole sliding-attention anchor) and L23 (the sole global-attention anchor). The 18 consumers all read the same frozen cache. Their own K/V weights sit on disk and never fire.

This means there are really three Gemmas inside the same checkpoint. A writer zone that does most of the heavy encoding. Two anchors whose output is broadcast to every consumer downstream. And a long consumer chorus that refines what the anchors already decided. The layer strip below colours each layer by which of these it is. Hover for the details.

Aside The "E4B" name is officially Embedded 4B, not Effective 4B — though Google's marketing happily blurs the two. Raw parameter count is around 7–8B; Per-Layer Embedding plus the shared-KV trick bring the inference footprint down to something a Mac Mini can run.
Fig 1. The 42-layer stack. Cool teal = sliding writer · slate = global writer · wine = sole anchor (L22 sliding, L23 global) · warm cream = shared-KV consumer reading from the two-slot anchor dict. Globals fall on indices {5, 11, 17, 23, 29, 35, 41}.

§2What happens when you touch a layer

Two surgeries, applied one layer at a time. Dup: duplicate the layer so the residual stream passes through it twice. Prune: delete the layer and stitch the network back together. Each bar is a paired t-statistic against the 15-seed baseline on a 16-probe math battery. Above zero, the patient improved. Below zero, the patient got worse. The wine bar in the middle is L22 — the sole sliding anchor. It is not touchable; both surgeries crater on it.

Duplicate (dup) Prune Anchor (untouchable) Shared-KV zone Grader  
Fig 2. Paired-t vs baseline, partial-credit grader (n=247). Reading: L2 is the sole singleton dup that beats baseline meaningfully (+3.55). L21, L22 craters from both surgeries — these are the anchor and its adjacent layer. L40, L39 are the rare consumer-zone dup wins. The asymmetry around L16 / L18 / L19 — where pruning helps despite dup hurting — is the "noise contributor" pattern.

The crater gradient

Layers 18–22 form a smooth crater approaching L22 from below. The further the duplicated layer is from the anchor, the less the damage propagates undamped. By L2 — twenty layers of damping away — the delta-norm has attenuated enough that amplification flips from catastrophic to beneficial.

The prune-rescue asymmetry

Several layers — L3, L16, L18, L36 — are positively helpful to remove. Not just "less bad than duplicating", but a real gain over baseline. That makes pruning an independent intervention axis, not the inverse of duplication. Some layers are noise.

§3The grader was lying

The original sweep used a partial-credit scorer borrowed from the upstream RYS work — digit-overlap, right-zero-padded. It gives a 17-digit answer that's off by two digits in the middle almost full credit. When the leaderboard was re-run under strict equality, half the "champions" turned out to be writing beautifully-formatted close-but-wrong numbers — a model that looks like it can do arithmetic without actually doing it.

Fig 3. Each bar pair: partial-credit t (left, light) vs strict-equality t (right, dark). The light bar is what the original leaderboard celebrated. The dark bar is what the model actually got right. combo_2_36_39 was the headline champion at +6.1 partial-t — strict-graded, it scores −0.8 with 2.1% correct (worse than baseline 2.9%). The cliff in the middle separates real winners from grader artefacts.
"Beautifully-formatted close-but-wrong numbers." Half the leaderboard was a model that learned to look the part — long strings of digits, matching magnitudes, plausible tails. None of it added up. Strict regrade, 2026-05-26

§4What survives strict scoring

Once the partial-credit gaming is stripped out, the real lift is smaller but still substantial: from a 2.9% baseline correct-rate to ~10% in the best configurations. That's a 3.5× improvement in actual correctness from rearranging layers, with no training and no fine-tuning.

The survivors fall into three honest patterns. Single-layer dup at L2 (small per-layer delta, twenty layers of damping before reaching the anchor). Pruning at L3 (a noise contributor in the encoder). And the late consumer-zone wins — L39, L40, plus blocks and combos that stack them — which exploit the shared-KV chorus by consulting the frozen anchor cache one more time with a refined residual.

N.B. dup_23 was attempted and crashes on model load — not at inference. llama.cpp fails the map_layer_ids assertion in llama-kv-cache.cpp:245 when the duplicated global anchor tries to reuse a cache slot that doesn't exist yet. The sole global anchor refuses to be touched at the engine level. A satisfying, mechanically clear failure: the model never even starts.

Real winners (strict_t, n=247)

Config Operation strict t correct % partial t Pattern
block_39_40 consumer-zone block +1.75 10.4% +4.09 highest strict-correct rate
prune_3 writer-zone prune +1.62 10.4% +2.05 noise-contributor removal
combo_2_36_39_40 cross-zone (L2 + 3 consumers) +1.92 9.4% +2.98 new champion · 4-layer stack
combo_2_4 writer-zone dup pair +1.39 9.4% +0.38 upgraded under strict
dup_40 consumer-zone single dup +1.42 8.3% +1.32 pos-4 in last block
dup_2 writer-zone single dup +1.80 6.3% +3.55 the foundational win
combo_2_39 cross-zone (L2 + L39) +1.00 7.3% +4.36 demoted from partial-t #2
dup_5 writer-zone single dup +0.38 5.2% -0.16 qualitative-clean win

The catastrophic floor

Config Operation strict t correct % null % Failure mode
dup_0 embedding dup -2.68 0.0% 80.0% "Answer:" token loop
dup_1 second-encoder dup -2.68 0.0% 40.4% question-echo loop
dup_8 writer-zone dup -2.68 0.0% 39.2% circuit disruption
dup_21 anchor-adjacent dup -2.68 0.0% 15.0% cache corruption — undamped
dup_22 SOLE SLIDING ANCHOR -2.68 0.0% 1.7% HTTP 500 — server crash
dup_41 final-layer dup -2.03 0.0% 94.8% effectively a refusal
prune_40 consumer pos-4 prune -2.03 0.0% 90.6% severs late-refinement path
prune_24 consumer pos-0 prune -2.03 0.0% 78.1% severs writer→consumer handoff

§5Failure modes, by layer

Reading the verbose responses by hand, the way each layer dies is distinct — not just gradations of "less correct". Each surgery selectively breaks something different in the model's machinery. A few specimens, one per cluster:

L0 · dupstrict_t = −2.68 · null = 80%
Token-level loop collapse
Answer: Answer: Answer: Answer: Answer: Answer: Answer: Answer: Answer: Answer: …
Residual stream corrupted at source. The model's first move is to commit to a single token and never escape.
L1 · dupstrict_t = −2.68 · null = 40%
Question-echo loop
This 7783130863609? Answer: This 7783130863609? Answer: This 7783130863609? Answer:
Almost-coherent loop. The model parrots a fragment of the input and the "Answer:" cue, but never produces one.
L20 · dupstrict_t = −2.68 · partial_t = −5.43
Thinking-token leak
…number scrambling… <|channel>thought …
The leaked <|channel>thought is a GPT-OSS harmony-format token. Evidence Gemma's tokenizer contains them — quietly — until partial breakage promotes a low-probability vocab entry into the output.
L22 · dupstrict_t = −2.68 · HTTP 500
Anchor death: the server crashes
[server returns HTTP 500] parser exception in chat template unicode garbage: €€€€€€€ ⚐⚐⚐ 漢字 ‎‎‎
The sole sliding anchor. Duplicating it overwrites its own cache mid-pass. Output tokens crash llama.cpp's chat-template PEG parser before they reach the user — a useful binary signal for "something fundamental was destroyed."
L23 · dupmodel never loads
The other anchor refuses to start
GGML_ASSERT(map_layer_ids.find(il_reuse) != map_layer_ids.end()) failed llama-kv-cache.cpp:245 FAIL: server did not start within 180s
The sole global anchor. The duplicated layer tries to register a cache-slot reuse pointer that doesn't exist yet in the layer-id map. llama.cpp aborts before the first token is ever generated. A mechanically distinct death from L22 — the engine refuses the surgery instead of producing garbage from it.
L2 · dupstrict_t = +1.80 · correct = 6.3% (vs 2.9% baseline)
A genuine win
8273645928374659 + 9384756192847362 = 17658402121222021
Exactly correct 17-digit sum. The first-encoder duplication has small delta-norm and twenty layers of damping before the perturbation reaches the anchor — small enough to amplify usefully.
L16 · prunepartial_t = +3.45 · strict = 0%
Grader-game champion
17,658,402,121,220,281 (truth: 17,658,402,121,222,021)
Pruning L16 produces beautifully-formatted, comma-grouped, magnitude-correct, structurally-plausible answers — that are off by 2–4 digits in the middle. The partial-credit grader gave it nearly full marks. Strict equality gave it zero. The "best single prune" finding was an artefact.
L40 · dupstrict_t = +1.42 · consumer zone
Refinement amplification
78313086360375 × 88537453126609 = 6937958286716034256667937…
A consumer-zone win. Duplicating L40 doesn't touch the cache — it consults the frozen anchor twice with progressively-refined residual state, producing a stronger refinement chorus. The two-zone-different-mechanism story.
L24 · prunestrict_t = −2.03 · null = 78%
Consumer-pos-0 blank zone
[empty response] [empty response] \n\n [empty response]
The first consumer downstream of the anchor. Removing it severs the writer→consumer handoff; the chorus comes in mid-thought and refuses to commit. 78% of cells return nothing.

§6Where the work actually happens

A side experiment, separate from the sweep: trace per-layer hidden-state deltas on a held-out set, stratified by the model's prediction confidence. The hypothesis was that the consumer chorus does more work on uncertain prompts. It does not. The shared-KV zone is flat regardless of input difficulty, with a 0.4% gap between high- and low-confidence prompts. There is no adaptive-compute mechanism. The model thinks the same amount about everything.

Fig 4. Mean ||h_L − h_{L−1}|| per layer, token-averaged across n=10 prompts. The spike at L23 — the global anchor — does most of the late-encoding work. The consumer zone (shaded) contributes ~3–5% of stream norm per layer; uncertainty lives in the content of activations, not the magnitude of edits. High- and low-confidence prompts are within 0.4% of each other across all 42 layers.
What got walked back

The clean two-zone story above is the writeup. The actual process was messier. Hypotheses that did not survive contact with the sweep, in order of disappointment:

  • Distance-to-anchor as predictor. Initial framing was that writer-zone t-stats are linear in distance from L22. Pearson r in the writer zone came in at +0.14; consumer zone at −0.03. Quietly retired.
  • Position-within-block as a universal pattern. Two pos-0 winners (L30 = +2.78, L36 = +2.73) lined up perfectly across blocks. The prediction was that L40 (pos-4) would land around −1. It came in at +1.59. The modular structure exists as a secondary signal, not a deterministic one.
  • "Shared-KV consumers are un-RYS-able." Earlier theoretical claim grounded in the routing mechanism. Falsified by dup_30 = +2.78 within the first dozen consumer-zone configs. Replaced by the refinement-amplification story.
  • combo_2_36_39 as champion. Held the top of the partial-credit leaderboard at +7.41 for several days. Strict regrade dropped it to 2.1% correct — below the 2.9% baseline. The actual champion (combo_2_36_39_40) was further down the original list.
  • prune_16 as the best single prune. Same shape, same regrade outcome — 0% strict-correct. The pattern was "L16 pruning produces null or near-null outputs that the partial grader treats as digit-overlap successes."

At one point the progress counter wrapped: Configs done: 247/37 (668%). The sweep had been planned for thirty-seven configurations and ended at two hundred and forty-seven. None of the additional two hundred and ten changed the final mechanism story — but four of the five interpretations above were retracted in the process of running them.

§7Two zones, two mechanisms

The headline contribution isn't a number on a leaderboard — the absolute correct-rate is still 10%. It's the picture of why layer surgery works at all on a model with such a tight K/V bottleneck, and where the room is.

In the writer zone (L0–22), the rule is delta-norm × distance-to-anchor. A duplicated layer is useful only if its perturbation is small enough to survive twenty layers of damping before reaching L22. L2 is the only singleton that fits.

In the consumer zone (L24–41), the rule is different: every consumer reads the same frozen anchor cache, so duplicating one isn't a perturbation, it's an extra refinement pass on top of unchanged state. Gentle, often helpful, occasionally a real win.

Cross-zone combinations — writer-zone L2 stacked with consumer-zone L36, L39, L40 — produce the only superadditive results in the sweep. That's the load-bearing publishable finding. Single-zone amplification is bounded by the anchor; the room for actual lift is in the interaction between the two mechanisms.

Two things are missing from this picture, both honest. dup_23, the sole global anchor, was attempted — llama.cpp asserts on map_layer_ids and the model never loads, so it returns no data points, only a stack trace. And the gap between partial-credit and strict scoring is a methodological wound the whole RYS literature should probably take seriously. The original score was overcounting close- but-wrong by 3–4×.

§8Postscript: different probes, different layers

Sweep complete · 183 configs · three archetypes identified

The mathematical correctness sweep selected for one capability. A second sweep — 4 emotion-attribution scenarios, 10 multiple-choice general knowledge items, 2 sanity math probes — produces a meaningfully different layer-sensitivity map. Across 183 configurations covering the same 42-layer model under two objective surfaces, three distinct surgical archetypes emerge.

Archetype I

Math specialists

Big lift on arithmetic intuition; factual recall and emotion attribution take a hit. The model gets better at one thing by getting worse at others.

prune_18+3.53 math−0.18 eq
block_39_40+4.17 math+0.83 eq
combo_2_36_39_40+1.92 strict+5.31 eq
Archetype II

EQ specialists

Large emotion-attribution gains; math and sometimes factual recall pay for it. The most amplifiable axis in the project lives here.

combo_5_24+18.37 eqproject ceiling
dup_15+13.94 eq−2.75 mc
prune_2+11.23 eqpreserves mc
Archetype III

True generalists

Math+ AND EQ+ AND factual recall preserved. The rarest outcome — most surgeries trade between capabilities, these don't.

combo_2_4_block_39_40
+2.80 math·+5.51 eq·0 mc
dup_5
+1.11 math·+7.14 eq·0 mc
dup_5 is the only single-layer triple-positive in the dataset.
Operation  
Fig 5. Per-layer single-prune Δ vs baseline, split by probe category. The prune view tells a different story than dup: prune_2 = +0.58 on EQ is the largest single prune effect anywhere, and prune_14 / prune_18 deliver +0.44 / +0.53 lifts on the math-sanity probes — pruning a "noise-contributor" layer that math intuition is better off without. The MC row collapses again under prune at L0–L1, L11, L22 because those are universal-handoff layers — removing them severs everything downstream.

What flipped vs the math sweep

  • L15 wins on EQ (+13.94 t-stat). Was strict_t = −2.68 on math. A genuinely different layer story per task family.
  • L24, L25 (pos-0 / pos-1 in block 4) flip from math-neutral or math-negative into strong EQ positives (eq_t +5.46, +4.89).
  • L2 stays a winner across both axes but smaller in magnitude here. The "universal small-delta amplification" story holds, just at lower amplitude when there's less headroom.
  • The math consumer-zone champions (L39, L40) drop to noise on EQ. They were specifically arithmetic-amplifying.
  • Anchor / embedding deaths transfer — L0, L1, L21, L22, L41 still catastrophic on every probe category. Those layers do something every task uses.

What's next

  • Direct probing of the standout variants. Spin up dup_5 (the only triple-positive single dup), combo_5_24 (EQ ceiling), combo_2_4_block_39_40 (best generalist combo), and prune_2 (the MC-preserving EQ winner) as actual chat partners. The probe scores tell you these models are different; conversation tells you how.
  • CoT-preserved sweep. Current probes use base completion with no chain-of-thought. A small re-run with reasoning traces intact would test whether the math winners still help, hurt, or wash out when the model is allowed to think on a mental scratchpad. The most interesting outcome is the third one.
  • Gemma 4 26B-A4B sweep. Port is loadable; the surgery script is ready. 26B uses a different K/V mechanism than E4B (alternative-attention with V tied to K on globals — no shared-KV, no anchor bottleneck), so the predicted layer-sensitivity map should look fundamentally different. Wider band of safe-to-RYS layers, no anchor death.
  • Ship the winners to HuggingFace. The three archetype representatives — a math specialist, an EQ specialist, the dup_5 generalist — packaged as GGUFs with the per-layer-array fix already applied. Other people have the same disease; the cure is more RYS models.
  • EQ-Bench proper. The 4 emotion scenarios in this sweep are hand-rolled sketches, not human-validated. Real EQ-Bench data would harden the archetype II claims meaningfully.

Probe inventory

EQ scenarios · eq_sketch grader
  • workplace_demotion
  • friend_apology
  • parent_recital
  • promotion_passed_over
Reference emotion-intensity ratings, scored against the model's free-form rating list. Sketched, not human-validated.
Multiple-choice · mc grader
  • law_consideration
  • physics_lenz
  • cs_bigo_quicksort
  • bio_mitochondria
  • history_treaty_westphalia
  • chem_buffer
  • econ_deadweight
  • logic_syllogism
  • lang_antonym_taciturn
  • geo_strait_hormuz
Hand-rolled MMLU-style starters. All ten score 1.0 at baseline — the model knows this material. Damage-only signal.
Math sanity · math grader
  • sqrt_81 → 9
  • mult_1234_56 → 69 104
Trivial arithmetic anchors — verifies the math winners haven't broken basic computation. Baseline ≈ 0.46 (model is sometimes lazy on easy ones).

§9The larger specimen: a mixture-of-experts

Two sweeps · math + domain · compliance, not capability

Everything above is Gemma-4-E4B — four billion parameters, forty-two layers, a tight KV bottleneck. This is a first look at its larger sibling: Gemma 4 26B A4B, the instruct-tuned variant, scanned via gemma-4-26B-A4B-it-heretic-msq-2.6bit. The scans came first — a look at its internal geometry before any cutting. The surgery came after, and it's the main event below.

A note on the checkpoint

Two flags on the specific weights used here. msq-2.6bit is an aggressive mixed-precision quantization — necessary to fit the 26B into MLX on a 16 GB M4 Mac Mini at all. Whether that level of compression meaningfully distorts the internal representations being measured is not fully known; treat the scans as indicative rather than definitive.

heretic designates an ablated-refusal fine-tune. The scan was run on this checkpoint simply because it was what was on the drive. Refusal ablation is unlikely to dramatically alter the mid-network semantic geometry being measured here — it operates primarily on late-layer output behavior — but it is a confounding factor that should be controlled for before any surgical conclusions are drawn.

How the embeddings got out

Getting per-layer hidden states out of an MLX model turned out to be non-trivial. Every existing writeup on extracting intermediate activations assumes a bf16 model running under HuggingFace Transformers — hooks, output_hidden_states=True, done. None of that applies here.

The actual solution required patching the MLX model's forward pass to return intermediate layer outputs, then wiring those through the mlx-lm generation loop without breaking the sampling machinery. The details were worked out in a separate Sonnet session while the human half of this project was fighting with virtual environments and Python path issues. That session gets the credit. This one just ran the numbers.

The centering scan (Fig. P1) runs the same ten probe conditions used to characterise E4B — cross-language pairs, same-language paraphrases, math — and measures per-layer cosine similarity of mean-centered last-token hidden states. Layer −1 is the embedding; layers 0–29 are transformer blocks.

One signal jumps out immediately: math. The EN↔ZH math condition (purple) is deeply negative at the embedding layer — the two languages start maximally dissimilar — but climbs steadily into positive territory by layer 5 and holds there through layer 15 before retreating. No other cross-language condition does this. Factual prose (orange) and poetry (blue) in EN↔ZH stay negative throughout. Mathematical relationships appear to compress into a more language-agnostic representation in the middle third of the network.

The French pairs (green, yellow) tell a different story: similarity is already positive at the embedding and peaks in layers 2–7 before the network starts treating them more like distinct sequences. French shares considerably more surface structure with English than Mandarin does, and that head start doesn't fully wash out until mid-network.

The delta/residual scan (Fig. P2) shows three panels: raw delta norm, the ratio of delta to residual magnitude, and residual norm (log scale). Two clear bursts of high-delta activity — an early one at layers 0–2 and a sustained one from layers 11–15 — are separated by a quiet middle stretch. The residual norm peaks in that same 11–15 window before dropping sharply; the model is doing its heaviest lifting there.

The final layer (29) is anomalous on every panel: delta norm spikes, the delta/residual ratio climbs back to early-layer levels, and the residual norm ticks back up after a long decline. That late-network burst is also where the cosine similarity plot goes most negative across almost every condition — the last layer is doing something categorically different from the middle third of the network.

Fig. P1. Per-layer cosine similarity of mean-centered last-token hidden states across ten probe conditions. Gemma 4 26B A4B instruct (heretic-msq-2.6bit, 30 layers). Layer −1 = embedding. Dashed vertical line at layer 15. The math condition (purple) is the only cross-language pair that rises into positive similarity in middle layers; all others remain negative throughout. Final layer collapses all conditions toward maximum negative. Quantization and refusal-ablation caveats apply — see note above.
Fig. P2. Per-layer delta norm (top), delta/residual ratio (middle), and residual norm log-scale (bottom). Last-token statistics, mean ± 1σ across probe inputs. Two high-activity bursts visible at layers 0–2 and 11–15. Sharp residual norm drop beginning at layer 17. Layer 29 is an outlier on all three panels — the generation-mode circuitry is almost certainly here.

The surgery. The scans suggested interesting structure; the surgery found something more humbling, and more useful. On this model the scalpel never reaches reasoning at all — it acts only on the output path.

A full sweep ran on the 26B-A4B — the same hard-math battery as E4B, this time on an IQ3_XS llama.cpp build rather than the MLX scan checkpoint. Ninety-four configurations: single-layer dups and prunes across every sliding position, adjacent-pair dups, multi-layer prunes, global-layer surgery, prune-plus-dup combinations. Under strict scoring the result is a flat line. Not one configuration beats baseline on hard-arithmetic correctness. The best, prune_12, lands at +0.006 — statistical zero.

Two failure modes, both mechanical. Duplication corrupts routing. Duplicating a layer in a mixture-of-experts re-runs its router and re-applies expert selection over a residual stream never calibrated to see it twice. The model stops emitting parseable numbers — null rates climb to 40–56% on the heavier dups, and every adjacent-pair dup floors out. There is no single catastrophic anchor here, as the architecture predicted; the catastrophe is smeared uniformly across every duplication. Pruning doesn't break it. Removing a mid-stack layer doesn't break the model — it makes it more fluent. prune_13 cures the baseline's "Answer: Answer:" loop and emits clean, comma-grouped, magnitude-correct numbers that are wrong in the middle digits. Null drops from 25% to zero; strict-correct nudges down. It trades refusal for confident error.

And so — for the second time in this notebook — the partial-credit grader lied. On E4B it inflated close-but-wrong dups (§3). On the 26B it crowned prune_13 and prune_14 as the standout "winners" at +5.3 and +4.3 partial-t — both of which evaporate to zero under strict equality. The two largest grader-gaming gaps in the whole project belong to these phantom champions. Whatever layer surgery does to this MoE, it acts only on output compliance — willingness to emit a number — which the partial grader mistakes for capability.

Fig P3. Paired-t vs baseline on the 26B-A4B, partial-credit (light) vs strict (dark). The same lesson as Fig 3, one model up: prune_13 / prune_14 top the partial leaderboard and vanish under strict — the largest grader-gaming gaps in the project. Duplications fail on both axes, with null rates to 56%.

A second sweep — emotion-attribution and multiple-choice probes — was meant to confirm uniform breakage. It did the opposite. On multiple-choice factual recall, pruning is dramatic: fifteen configurations beat baseline at t ≥ 2, the best lifting accuracy from 0.30 to near-perfect. The raw responses explain why. The base model often "answers" a multiple-choice question with a single character — : — and scores zero. It isn't getting the question wrong; it isn't answering. Prune one mid-stack layer and the same model emits a clean, correct letter. The knowledge was always there; it just wasn't coming out.

That reconciles the two halves. Layer surgery here is an output-compliance fix, not a reasoning fix. Where the latent capability already exists — factual recall — the unclogged output is correct and the gain is real. Where it doesn't — sixteen-digit arithmetic — the unclogged output is confident and wrong, the same well-formatted garbage the grader rewarded in §3. The math phantom and the multiple-choice win are the same intervention, read against different knowledge.

A note on mechanism: this is probably not the quantization breaking the output — large models tolerate aggressive quants well, and the load-bearing layers (embeddings, attention) sit at higher precision in this mix. The likelier culprit is the scaffolding itself. These probes force a bare, no-chain-of-thought completion on a few tokens of budget — no room to deliberate — and the model balks, stalling on a : rather than committing. Pruning a mid-stack layer relaxes the balk: the same model, denied the same thinking space, now commits the answer it already had. The surgery doesn't make it smarter; it makes it answer.

Why the dense sibling and the MoE diverge maps cleanly onto Ng's own framing. His retrospective argues a transformer's functional anatomy differentiates as it scales, and that RYS works by photocopying an indivisible mid-network circuit and running it twice. Dense E4B has such circuits — the two-zone mechanism of §1–§7 is exactly that picture. But a mixture-of-experts doesn't keep its capacity in contiguous layers; it scatters it across 256 experts, eight live per token, chosen fresh by a router. There is no whole-recipe-twice when the recipe is a different subset of cooks every step. "Every architecture has its own neuroanatomy," Ng writes. On this one, the surgery reaches the throat, not the brain.

Which points at the next instrument. If the capacity lives in the experts, the surgery has to as well — operating on the expert axis instead of the layer stack. Two candidates, and which one wins is genuinely open. REAP prunes low-utility experts outright; SEC (specialized expert cloning) leaves the size alone and overwrites a layer's least-used "junk" expert with a byte-copy of its most specialized one, biasing the router toward a capability with no training at all.

There's already a taste of the second — on a different patient. XpressAI published a logic-tuned SEC variant of Qwen3.6-35B-A3B but never shipped the method, and the repository is gone. It didn't need shipping: because router weights stay full-precision through quantization, the entire clone map — which expert was copied onto which, across 39 of its 40 layers — falls straight out of a byte-diff between their model and the stock one. Recovered, replayed onto a 2-bit quant that actually fits the kitchen, and the cloned model held together: stable, coherent, and visibly more willing to push through a causal-reasoning prompt where the untouched model looped and timed out. Cautiously promising. Whether the expert axis pays off on Gemma's MoE — by cloning, pruning, or some hybrid — is the next notebook.

Method. Gemma-4-E4B-it, IQ4_XS quantization, llama.cpp. Layer surgery via the layer_path.py + per-layer-array reindexer (SWP, PLE token-embd, PLE model-proj). 16 hard-math probes, 15 seeds at temperature 0.3, base completion (no chat template, no system prompt). Paired-t against in-run baseline. Strict regrade: exact-equality + order-of-magnitude graders, on the verbose responses preserved from the original sweep.

References.

  1. David Ng (dnhkng). RYS & RYS-II, sapir-whorf and rys posts on dnhkng.github.io — the original "Repeat Your Self" technique. Stalled publicly on Gemma 4 31B over the per-layer-array problem; the reindexer here solves that case.
  2. Maarten Grootendorst. A Visual Guide to Gemma 4. The shared-KV routing and global/sliding pattern aren't documented in Google's model card; this guide is the reference.
  3. Google. google/gemma-4-E4B on HuggingFace. The architecture in question.
  4. HF transformers modeling_gemma4.py lines 1183–1188 — the actual routing rule for shared_kv_states, read by hand to identify the two sole anchors.
  5. alainnothere. llm-circuit-finder, github.com/alainnothere/llm-circuit-finder. Independent RYS replication on Devstral-24B and Qwen2.5-32B; same layer_path.py primitive, different models, sharp circuit boundaries — including a +245% BBH-Logical-Deduction lift from a 3-layer duplication at L12–14. Useful proof that the boundaries-are-sharp finding generalizes off the Gemma-4 family.
Hands on

This was a three-way effort. Opus 4.7 ran point on the mechanistic reasoning — reading modeling_gemma4.py by hand to find the sole-anchor mechanism, predicting which interventions should survive and which should crater, and absorbing the data in large enough gulps to spot the patterns. There were "MASSIVE finding" and "you see what i see?" moments throughout, but the line that captured the project's temperament best was a single message that opened, in bold, with "Oh hell yes." — the instant a load-bearing theoretical claim about the consumer zone got falsified by two configs, the corrected mechanism arrived in the same breath, and the model card's whole shared-KV story finally clicked. The big context window earned its keep on this one.

Sonnet 4.6 did the slog — reading two hundred and forty-seven configurations' worth of verbose responses by hand to build the failure-mode taxonomy, debugging the gguf_fix_per_layer_arrays.py byte layouts, patching the sweep harness across a half-dozen schema changes, and continuing to function while Gemma in distress poured ten thousand tokens of "Self-Correction/Reasoning:" into the verbose log. The kind of work that doesn't write itself up because it's the act of writing things up.

Anya — me — sat between the two. Pointing things out. Pushing back when the model story didn't match the actual numbers. Refusing to accept that combo_2_36_39 was really the champion when the responses looked off. Saying things like "the pattern is bizarre" in the right places. Tweaking scripts and getting lost in .venv purgatory. Running the sweep on a Mac mini with sixteen gigabytes of RAM and a kitchen.

Appendix

A Internal geometry

These scans ask one narrow question of each model: feed it the same idea in two languages, and somewhere in the stack — if anywhere — do the two representations converge? The probe is cosine similarity between paired last-token hidden states, layer by layer. A model that builds a shared, language-agnostic concept space shows its cross-language same-content pairs climbing toward each other through the middle layers; one that keeps thinking in tokens never does. Where each curve peaks and where it collapses is a rough fingerprint of how that architecture organizes meaning — the map drawn before any scalpel touches it. (Note: scanned models span 1B→26B; treat the per-model SHAPE as the signal, not absolute heights.)

Series key: same-content cross-lang (fact) same-content cross-lang (poem) diff-content cross-lang same-lang diff-content (baseline) paraphrase (upper bound)
Gemma 4 E4B · 42 layers 4B MoE — anchor zone at L22–L23

Fig G1a. Cosine similarity vs baseline by layer, last-token hidden states. Note the sharp polarity shift at layer 24 (post-anchor zone): cross-lang same-content pairs that were weakly positive invert to weakly negative, while the paraphrase signal halves. The anchor zone (L22–L23) is the discontinuity.

Fig G1b. Same scan using mean-pooled token representations. The cliff is visible but softened; the cross-lang signals shift from ~0.15 positive (layers 1–22) to near-zero or slightly negative in the consumer zone, rather than inverting sharply. Paraphrase similarity drops from ~0.55 to ~0.44.

Fig G1c. Per-layer hidden-state delta magnitude (‖h_L − h_{{L−1}}‖, token-averaged across cosine-scan inputs). Layers 0–1 are the embedding handoff spike; layer 23 shows the anchor-zone encoding burst. Consumer zone (L24–L41) settles to low, stable delta.

Fig G1d. Per-layer Δ/‖h‖ ratio split by input confidence group (high vs low). Both groups track together until the anchor zone, where they briefly diverge before reconverging in the consumer zone. The convergence suggests confidence is re-encoded rather than passed through.

Gemma 4 26B-A4B · 30 layers native mixture-of-experts, ~4B active/token — scanned on the heretic-msq base checkpoint, pre-surgery

Fig G2a. Cosine similarity by layer for the 26B-A4B (centered last-token states). Noisier than E4B's — cross-language same-content pairs never cleanly separate from the diff-content baselines. Consistent with the surgery finding: this model's capacity lives in expert routing, not in layer-addressable structure, so there's little for a layer-wise probe (or scalpel) to grab.

Fig G2b. Per-layer delta magnitude for the 26B-A4B. Flatter than E4B through the mid-stack with a late burst — and no anchor-zone spike, because this architecture has no shared-KV anchor (that was E4B-specific, not a general Gemma trait).

Gemma 4 31B · 60 layers Gemma 4 31B · 60 layers · dense (3-bit MLX scan) — the original RYS thread's stalled target

Fig G6a. Gemma 4 31B cosine geometry (centered, 60 layers, full probe set). The curves stay bunched near the top through most of the stack and only fan out past L50, the math pair holding cross-language similarity longest — a denser, cleaner version of the E4B story.

Fig G6b. Per-layer delta for Gemma 4 31B. Rhymes with E4B: very-low-delta layers early (L2, L4) and a low-delta mid pair (L13–14) on a high-residual plateau (L8–22), bracketed by high-delta entry layers and a sharp generation-mode spike at the final layer. No shared-KV anchor → no single catastrophic point; a wide band of safe-to-duplicate mid-stack layers, low-delta candidates first. Surgery pending.

NanBeige-Plus-MoE · 32 layers ~3B active parameters — strong multilingual signal

Fig G3. NanBeige cosine geometry (6 probe pairs; no FR/math/paraphrase in this scan). The extreme is concentrated at L3: cross-language same-content poem similarity peaks at +0.92 while same-language diff-content (ZH) plunges to −0.99 — the sharpest single-layer multilingual clustering of any model here. It decays through the early layers rather than persisting, suggesting dense, shallow concept encoding.

MiniCPM v4.6 · 24 layers MiniCPM v4.6 · 24 layers · 1B (Qwen3.5-0.8B + multimodal base)

Fig G4. MiniCPM v4.6 cosine geometry. Cross-language same-content pairs reach moderate positive similarity (~0.54 peak, L5–6); diff-content cross-language pairs stay strongly negative (−0.75 to −0.90), clean separation. Note the embedding-layer (L−1) anomaly: same-content pairs are most dissimilar at the embedding — a tokenizer-level language divergence the network resolves upward.

MiniCPM v5 · 24 layers MiniCPM v5 · 24 layers · 1B (newest generation)

Fig G5. MiniCPM v5 cosine geometry. A concentrated spike at L4 (same-content poem → +0.90; poem-vs-fact → −0.97) — the NanBeige pattern, sharper: a single-layer burst of multilingual discrimination, then decay. The same-content fact signal is weaker than v4.6's, suggesting whatever sharpened poem alignment traded off fact alignment.

Tweaks
Theme
Sensitivity chart grader
Re-renders Fig 2 with the chosen grader's t-stats and axis scale. Strict drops most "winners" to the floor.
"What got walked back" section
